aliases: Canned sea coconut with crushed ice

english: Canned sea coconut with crushed ice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sea coconut dessert; mapped to broader dessert food product class. Sea coconut (Lodoicea maldivica fruit) is a niche ingredient not individually represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sweet dishes and desserts) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for sea coconut dessert in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for sea coconut dessert or sea coconut as a food ingredient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sea coconut dessert is approximately 250 g, served as one bowl of crushed ice topped with canned sea coconut pieces and sugar syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Sea Coconut Dessert — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Sea Coconut Dessert (cr_id F02020012). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sea coconut dessert in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sugar-syrup-based Asian desserts with similar sugar content (~9g/100g) and low fibre (<0.5g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (34.2g from 250g serving) / 100 = 22.3. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (9.06g/100g) with negligible fibre (0.39g/100g); low sodium (5mg/100g) is a positive factor but does not offset the sugar-driven metabolic concern
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is low at 5mg/100g and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, the high added sugar content (9.06g/100g) with minimal fibre contributes to insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome, which are independent cardiovascular risk factors in hypertensive individuals — limit portion to 100g or request an unsweetened version.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (9.06g/100g) from added sugar syrup with negligible dietary fibre (0.39g/100g), resulting in rapid glucose absorption and estimated medium-to-high glycemic load per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sea coconut dessert contains 9.06g sugar per 100g with only 0.39g fibre, producing a glycemic load of approximately 22 per 250g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dessert to a maximum 50g portion, and should request the sea coconut flesh without added sugar syrup to reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat is effectively zero (0.0g/100g confirmed by HPB source) and sodium is low (5mg/100g), which are positive factors; however, added sugar at 9.06g/100g with negligible fibre raises triglyceride and inflammatory risk with regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sea coconut dessert is very low in fat and sodium, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the added sugar content (9.06g/100g) can elevate triglycerides and promote low-grade inflammation with habitual consumption — limit to occasional small portions and prefer unsweetened preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea coconut dessert is a popular Singaporean chilled sweet dish consisting of crushed ice topped with pieces of canned sea coconut (Lodoicea maldivica, also known as coco de mer), typically served with sugar syrup and optionally with jelly. Sea coconut flesh is translucent, firm yet gelatinous in texture, and mildly sweet with a subtle floral flavour. The dessert is commonly sold at hawker centres and dessert stalls across Singapore and is a staple of the local tong sui (sweet soup/dessert) tradition. Per 100g, it provides 55 kcal, 13.69g carbohydrate (of which 9.06g is sugar from added syrup), 0.19g protein, effectively 0g fat, 0.39g dietary fibre, and 5mg sodium. Calcium is 3mg and iron is 0.06mg per 100g. The dessert is plant-based, dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. Clinically, the high added sugar content relative to its negligible fibre makes it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome without portion control. The low sodium and zero fat profile are favourable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, but the sugar load remains a concern. It is best consumed occasionally and in small portions by those with metabolic health conditions.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat sea coconut dessert?
How many calories in sea coconut dessert?
Is sea coconut dessert suitable for hypertension?
What is coco de mer dessert?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02020012) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this specific dessert.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source (F02020012); sea coconut flesh and sugar syrup contain no measurable fat, and saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are also confirmed zero by formulation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this food and are not available in other approved sources for this specific preparation as of 2026. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on comparable sugar-syrup-based Asian desserts from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement for sea coconut dessert is not available. Glycemic load per 250g serving = 65 × 34.2 / 100 = 22.3.
